MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
Off Calendar Jan-06-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MARC PRICE WOLF, Esq. of the San Francisco City Attorney's Office (Tel: 415-554-3901) for Plaintiffs City and County of San Francisco and People of the State of California;
Atty. PAUL MCCARTHY, Esq. of Law Offices of Beles & Beles (Tel: 510-836-0100) for Defendant Angelica Bato.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT ANGELICA BATO's Motion to Set Aside the Default Entered on August 22, 2019.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Motion DENIED. A court has inherent equitable power to set aside a default outside the six-month jurisdictional period in which relief can be obtained under section 473(b). (See Austin v. Los Angeles Unified School Dist. (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 918, 918 ["The six-month time limitation is jurisdictional; the court has no power to grant relief under section 473 once the time has lapsed."].) The Court rejects Defendant's contention that the default must be set aside for "extrinsic fraud or mistake." Even if Defendant's showing were adequate to show that her prior counsel engaged in positive misconduct amounting to a complete abandonment of the attorney-client relationship, she does not satisfy two essential requirements for relief on that ground. First, she does not show that she has a meritorious defense. (Lee v. An (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 558, 566.) Second, Defendant did not act with diligence in seeking to set aside the default once it was discovered. (Id.; see also Aldrich v. San Fernando Valley Lumber Co. (1985) 170 Cal.App.3d 725, 739 ["[t]he client's own negligence in following up and pursuing [her] case is also scrutinized, even in cases of positive misconduct on the part of the attorney."].) While Defendant claims that she did not learn that a default had been entered against her until after Mr. Beles became her attorney (Bato Decl. par. 4), that contention is belied by the Court's docket, which establishes that she was served with the default in August 2019, which was mailed both to her home and business addresses as well as emailed to her counsel. In any event, Mr. Beles states that he was first approached by Ms. Bato in June 2020. (Beles Decl. par. 2.) Defendant Bato did not file the instant motion until November 24, 2020-more than 5 months later, and more than 15 months after her default was entered. By any measure, that unexplained lengthy delay establishes lack of diligence on Bato's part. (See, e.g., Pulte Homes Corp. v. Williams Mechanical, Inc. (2016) 2 Cal.App.5th 267, 277-278 [trial court abused its discretion in granting motion to set aside default judgment where defendant delayed four and one-half months seeking relief and therefore "did not show diligence"].)

Order signed.
